sections.title: Overview
sections.content: High-risk mental health presentations involve imminent risk of harm to self or others, severe behavioural disturbance, loss of reality testing, or inability to care for basic needs. Examples include suicidal crisis, severe agitation, psychosis, violent behaviour, or severe self-neglect.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Suicidal ideation, recent self-harm, or suicide attempt
- Severe agitation, aggression, paranoia, or psychosis
- Marked disorganisation, bizarre behaviour, or loss of insight
- Catatonia or profound withdrawal
- Inability to maintain basic safety or self-care

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Personal safety and bystander safety come first
- Always consider organic causes of behavioural disturbance such as hypoxia, hypoglycaemia, intoxication, overdose, TBI, seizure, stroke, infection, or delirium
- Use the least restrictive approach that is safe and practicable
- Positive RASS alone does not mandate sedation
- Physical restraint and sedation are high-risk interventions and require clear justification, close monitoring, and documentation

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Ensure scene safety, remove hazards where possible, and request police or additional resources early if needed
- Primary Survey and Vital Sign Survey, including BGL and SpO2 where possible
- Use calm, non-confrontational communication and verbal de-escalation
- Do not leave a high-risk suicidal patient alone if it is unsafe to do so
- If immediate physical restraint is required to prevent serious harm, use the minimum force and duration necessary within local policy
- Escalate early to senior clinical support or the on-call doctor when risk, capacity, or disposition is unclear

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Address reversible medical contributors as soon as practicable
- In the prehospital setting, a paramedic may decide on clinical grounds to sedate a patient when the patient poses an immediate danger to themselves or others and less restrictive measures have failed or are unsafe
- Any sedation must remain within local scope, training, and protocol, with clinician judgment based on the whole presentation rather than a score alone
- After sedation, closely monitor airway, breathing, circulation, oxygenation, ventilation, temperature, and level of consciousness, and be prepared to manage complications

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Documentation should record the risk posed, capacity concerns, de-escalation attempts, why restraint or sedation was necessary, what was used, and the patient response
- Debrief and staff support may be appropriate after significant incidents
